replies.author: @gengis01
replies.text: Yeah, Tom Cruise's heroic moment in the ending was to point and scream "Look at the birds!  Look at the god-damn birds!"  So heroic.  So dramatic.  He doesn't shoot any alien.  He doesn't eject them into the vacuum of space, he doesn't load a virus into the alien mothership, he just points at the birds.  War of the Worlds needs to be rewritten for the modern age.  Back when it was written, alien life forms, advanced technology like heat rays and even the concept of viruses was all new and exotic.  Now the idea that a technologically advanced race that can travel between planets would succumb to viruses because while they have shields that can block missiles, they don't know about germs/viruses and do not have air scrubbers is ludicrous.  The 2005 version was also ludicrous.  This whole story line that the Martian placed the giant killing tripods millions of years ago and then zapped living pilots into it through lightning....the writers must have been stoned, high or drunk when they came up with that concept.  And it makes no difference at all to the plot.  But then nothing makes a difference, because, as pointed out, humanity has no effect on the outcome of the story -nothing except viruses has an effect on the outcome.  The sideplot about the son who ran off, the whiny daughter (whom I would have been tempted to sacrifice to the Martian invaders -has there ever been a more annoying, useless, helpless character in a movie?)

replies.author: @RogerCaplan
replies.text: @gengis01 The point of the original novel was to present that horror and powerlessness of being on the receiving end of an invasion to readers in a country that was nearly always the one doing the invading. It's like people in Africa facing a British or European millitary with weapons so advanced compared to their own that they have no hope of fighting back in any meaningful way. The biggest killer of European armies and colonists in the countries they conquered was disease - the efforts of the natives to resist them was feeble in comparison. It's not a story about fighting aliens, it's about the realitites of trying to survive in the face of an enemy that you can't defeat.

Back then the news was on a 12 to 24+ hour cycle, meaning that you rarely got anything in real time like we do now.

author: @NordicSnowhammer
text: Movies died in the early 10's.  Maybe even before that.  I miss the '70s through the '90s.  I miss going to the theater on a Saturday afternoon, sneaking in candy, and NOT paying a week's salary to do so.  I even miss standing in line for a heavily anticipated movie.  I miss tickets that a human actually tore in 2, then told you heartlessly which direction to turn and the number of your theater.  I miss the smell of gooey soda-soaked carpet.  Theater seats you hoped you'd get rather than the one you picked at the kiosk.  I miss good trailers that didn't tell you what the movie was about.  Sound pop when the audio played.  Film artifacts due to dirt or scratches.  The sound of a projector reel.  I basically miss everything that's missing from today's American Movie Theater experience.  Man, I'm getting old.

I miss the days when tickets were cheap, the anticipation of witnessing a cinematic gem made you stand in line, the hushed whispers when a trailer dropped, and the sound of rustling sweet bags. For me, nothing will ever top seeing Fellowship of the Ring as a wide-eyed and impressionable 17-year old in 2001 on the big screen for the first time in a packed auditorium that was devoid of social media, phones and TikTok, about to witness Tolkien’s classic tale come to life. Or the audience laughing in unison at Heath Ledger’s long and drawn out "Hiiiii…." while dressed up as a nurse in The Dark Knight as an entertained 24-year old. The movie world nose-dived after 2014. And it hasn’t gotten better. Now we live in a world of bloated comic book movies, sequels to classic IP’s nobody asked for, prequels, remakes and an over-reliance on CGI.

author: @eyespy3001
text:
At the height of the Covid lockdowns, I read an interview with director Timur Bekmemetov, the man who created the Russian Nightwatch/Daywatch movies, as well as the pretty good James McAvoy film, Wanted. In the interview, he was asked about how the future of filmmaking will look thanks to Covid (which didn’t have an end in sight at the time). His response was, “we’re currently developing a technology that will allow a director to be in one part of the world and direct an actor, like Ice Cube, who is at home, sitting in their closet doing a scene, and we’ll just add the background in in post. It’s very exciting and will revolutionize filmmaking.”

Well…I guess this is that movie.
